summary: Justin Brennan walks through updates to the Simple Deal Analyzer tool used for 0-50 unit multifamily deals, focusing on how to calculate and present investor returns in pitch decks. He explains waterfall calculations, cash-out refinance scenarios, the tax benefits of leverage, and how to provide two standardized investment examples ($25K and $100K) to potential capital partners.
takeaways: Provide only two investor examples on pitch decks: one at the minimum investment ($25,000) and one at six figures ($100,000), as statistically a ~$600K raise will have 4-5 investors at $25-50K and 2-3 at six figures
Keep hurdle one and two identical with 0% GP return and 100% LP return to avoid formula errors that incorrectly show equity paydowns

When doing a cash-out refi, each investor receives their pro-rata share based on their capital contribution percentage (e.g., if they invested $25K of $513K total, they get 5% of any refinance proceeds)
Operate 60-unit multifamily buildings off-site with a good PM within 10 miles; budget 6% management fee but model conservatively at 8% in analyzers to account for leasing commissions
Target a 30-40% return of capital within 36 months from refinance events as a realistic expectation; only attempt full payoff if market conditions align favorably

Use ChatGPT with specific prompts to compare markets: request population growth, job growth, and median household income for major cities over three years past and projected three years forward in a ranked table

Build broker relationships by consistent follow-up (couple touches per week) to access pre-market deals not listed online; fly in-person to meet brokers, local lenders, and property managers rather than relying solely on digital communication

skill_candidates.change: Learn to calculate investor return scenarios in the deal analyzer to accurately populate pitch deck examples
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin demonstrates how to input specific investment amounts ($25K, $100K) and use formulas to auto-calculate their equity multiplier, IRR, cash-on-cash return, and refinance proceeds; he emphasizes the waterfall tab shows how to pull exact numbers for pitch decks

skill_candidates.change: Understand cash-out refinance mechanics to model capital events and investor payback scenarios
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin walks through a real 31-unit example where they bought for $1.525M, valued it at $3.6M in 36 months, refinanced into $2.1M loan, paid back all investors, and kept $355K-$370K tax-free; he shows how to calculate pro-rata equity percentages for payback

skill_candidates.change: Use AI-driven market filtering to narrow geographic focus from states to specific high-growth cities before broker outreach
skill_candidates.evidence: When Shahan asks how to choose between North Carolina and Ohio, Justin directs him to ChatGPT with a specific prompt for population growth, job growth, median household income ranked over 6 years (3 back, 3 forward); he cites Columbus OH at 2-3% growth as an example signal

skill_candidates.change: Establish consistent broker follow-up cadence and in-person meetings to gain access to pre-market deal flow
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin emphasizes 'close mouths do not get fed' and recommends touching base with brokers a couple times per week with simple reachouts to signal you're a serious buyer; he stresses belly-to-belly meetings matter in commercial real estate because brokers control pre-market access that isn't online

title: Tuesday Mentorship Q&A - Mastering Real Estate Due Diligence_11-04-25 | Justin Brennan
